Description:
Camp doesn't have to end!  Extend your camp experience with our Allerton Kids Club programs during the school year.

Allerton Kids Club: Games Galore is a day-long program of games of all kinds for 1st through 4th graders.  Expect Minute-to-win-it challenges, puzzles, board games, card games, brain games, stem challenges, and a game of capture the flag.  Kids club attendees will also take a nature scavenger hunt hike in the morning.

Registration is $40 and space is limited. Your kiddo will need a sack lunch and refillable water bottle.  An afternoon snack will be provided.  Please note that part of the day will be spent outside, so please plan accordingly.

Registration is available to children in 1st, 2nd, 3rd and 4th grades (born between Sept 1, 2014 and Sept 1, 2017).
Categories: Allerton Programs > Youth All-Day Programs

Description:
In this workshop, participants will learn to make linoleum block art prints. The workshop will include an overview of the principles of relief printing, as well as instruction on safely using relief carving tools and making prints from carved linoleum blocks. Participants will recieve a linoleum block to carve and will take home both the block and the prints they make from it. Prints will dry overnight in the Studio and must be picked up during the following week.

Tools and materials will be provided. Pre-drawn fall and Halloween-themed designs will be available for those who wish to use them, but attendees are welcome to draw up their own creations!

This workshop is intended for ages 15 and up.

Instructor Bio

Felix Fuenty (they/them) is Allerton's newest program coordinator. They like cats, the library, spicy food, Halloween, being outside, and all kinds of art.
Categories:
The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art
Allerton Programs > Adult Programs

Description:
Saturday, Sept. 30

9 a.m. – 4 p.m.*

Music Barn

$90/person

Registration Deadline: September 15th

Experience level: Beginner

Open to students ages 14+

 

As we transition into fall, it becomes the season we tend to reflect on what we’re thankful for. This project, “Thankful”, starts by weaving a filled base on a 6” x 20” D handle. Techniques include start/stop weaving, twining, continuous weave, and the use of overlays. There will be an emphasis on shaping this basket. The accent colors are smoked, gold, and celery green. Shaker tape and sea grass are woven in this basket as accents creating lots of texture!  A colorful fall floral sprig and ribbon adorn the front with a pop of color in the rim. This basket is sure to become a favorite!

*Lunch will not be provided. Participants should bring a lunch or plan to visit the Greenhouse Cafe? during the break.

Registration fees include all materials. All sales are final.

 

Instructor Bio

Bonnie Rideout wove her first basket in 1994 and quickly caught the “basket bug”. In 2006 Bonnie started her business, “Bonnie’s Baskets” teaching others how to weave and led to selling basketry supplies, baskets and embellishments at craft and art shows. Shortly after, she began designing her own baskets, which she loves to do! In addition to teaching locally, Bonnie loves to travel to teach. Her travels have led her to Missouri, Michigan, Kentucky, Indiana, Florida, North Carolina, Tennessee, Iowa, Virginia, West Virginia, Ohio, Wisconsin, Kansas, Oklahoma, Texas, and Minnesota and is looking forward to adding a few more states to that list! In 2018 Bonnie returned to her basket business full time and is loving it! Her website is www.bonniesbasketsil.com. You can also find her on Facebook, Instagram and Etsy. Hope to see you on the “basket trail”!
Categories: The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art

Description:
Saturdays: 9/16, 9/23, 9/30, & 10/7 (students should be present for all classes)

10 a.m. – 12 p.m.

The Studio

$230/person total for all four classes

Registration Deadline: September 13th

Experience level: Beginner

Open to ages 12+; those under 15 must be accompanied by an adult.

 

Beginner Mountain Dulcimer class will provide basic instruction to allow students to start playing simple tunes. The class will start with a few basic notes and chords in DAD tuning and learn a few hymns and fiddle tunes on the mountain dulcimer.

 

What to bring: A mountain dulcimer, tuner, or tuner on a smartphone. Music stands will be provided. A limited quantity of loaner dulcimers is available.  Contact Lou Ann Koebel at Koebel@illinois.edu for availability prior to first class

 

Instructor Bio

Lou Ann has been playing the mountain dulcimer for several years and is involved in several local music jams. During the pandemic, she became involved in several Zoom music festivals and lessons to advance her playing. Currently, Lou Ann is in a band that plays weekly in assisted living homes to sing and play for residents.
Categories: The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art

Description:
Wednesdays: 9/13, 9/20, & 9/27

6 – 7 p.m.

The Studio

$50/person total for all three classes

Registration Deadline: September 8th

Experience level: Beginner

Open to all ages; those under 18 must be accompanied by an adult.

 

Participants will learn the basics of embroidery, from threading a needle and making the piece look good to embroidery stitches. The stitches taught will include backstitch, satin stitch, and cross stitch. French knots, and daisy or petal stitch may also be taught. A sampler, employing the stitches learned, will be started in the class. Students will need to bring a sharp pair of scissors to each class. All other materials will be provided.

 

Instructor Bio

Judy began embroidering as a child.  She has made samplers, both free-hand and following a printed pattern.  She has always enjoyed ‘needle arts.’  She has taught groups and individuals to embroider in a variety of settings.  As a retired teacher, she knows how to break down a complex task into easy steps and to explain the procedure well.
Categories: The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art

Description:
Mondays: 9/11, 9/18, 9/25, 10/2, 10/9, 10/16

5 - 7:30 p.m.

Music Barn

$235/person total for all six classes

Registration Deadline: September 6th

Experience level: Beginner

This 6-week class will encompass all aspects of Beginning Weaving starting with weaving terminology, learning parts of the loom, and correctly setting up the loom for a project among other important weaving skills and techniques. Participants of this class will create a table runner by the end of the 6-week class. Keep in mind that everyone learns differently, and some people may only be able to actively participate for 2 of the 2.5 hours per session, especially when learning something new with so many steps. Others may zip through the 2.5 hours and be ready to move on to the next step. Be bold and try something new!

Students are encouraged to bring their own loom to use for the duration of the class. Should you need to arrange to borrow a loom, please contact Sharon directly at weaveteach@gmail.com. 

 

Instructor Bio

Sharon has been weaving for 33 years and teaching beginning weaving for almost 32 years. She was taught to weave in a one-on-one class and wove 2 table runners in 12 approximately 2-hour lessons, and she teaches the same method when she teaches Sharon has experience in weaving with silk, lace weaving, 8 shaft pattern weaving, parallel weaving, block weaves, and Shaker reproduction towels. She has taught many workshops and classes to weavers’ guilds in Florida, Ohio, and Champaign-Urbana. She earned her Journeyman certification from the Weavers Guild of Boston in 2021 and a master’s certification in April of 2022.
Categories: The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art

Description:
Mondays: 10/23, 10/30, 11/6, 11/13, 11/20, 11/27

5 - 7:30 p.m.

Music Barn

$235/person total for all six classes

Registration Deadline: October 18th

Experience level: Beginner-Moderate

 

This will be a continuation of Beginning Weaving I*, but instead of teacher selection with everyone doing the same article, students will pick their own project, measure the warp, beam the warp, thread the heddles to their selected pattern, thread the reed, wind the bobbins and weave the project (table runner or mat, scarf, towel or other full size project).

Students are encouraged to bring their own loom to use for the duration of the class. Should you need to arrange to borrow a loom, please contact Sharon directly at weaveteach@gmail.com.

*Anyone who has a basic understanding of weaving and skills that were taught in Beginning Weaving I is welcome to join this class even if they did not take the first class at The Farms.

 

Instructor Bio

Sharon has been weaving for 33 years and teaching beginning weaving for almost 32 years. She was taught to weave in a one-on-one class and wove 2 table runners in 12 approximately 2-hour lessons, and she teaches the same method when she teaches Sharon has experience in weaving with silk, lace weaving, 8 shaft pattern weaving, parallel weaving, block weaves, and Shaker reproduction towels. She has taught many workshops and classes to weavers’ guilds in Florida, Ohio, and Champaign-Urbana. She earned her Journeyman certification from the Weavers Guild of Boston in 2021 and a master’s certification in April of 2022.

Description:
Please register for one spot for this class. Email Olivia at owarren@illinois.edu to let her know how many family members will be attending.

Wednesday, 9/13 & Saturday 9/16

9/13 – 6:00pm, ZOOM

9/16 – 12:00pm, Music Barn

$25/family

Registration Deadline: September 12th

Experience level: beginner, moderate, advanced

 

Ever wanted to go camping with your kids, but are unsure of where to start? Join Derek Peterson as he gives tips and tricks on how to camp with your kids. This two-day class will give you all the basics you need for your next family camping trip. 

On September 13th, join a Zoom discussion to talk about supplies that are needed, expectations prior to heading out, and pitfalls. 

On September 16th, join Derek as he leads the group on an afternoon hike around the south side of Allerton and share stories and experiences while hiking. You will camp out by the Music Barn and cook meals together over the fire. Sunday morning, share a big breakfast over the fire with fellow campers before heading home.

 

Instructor Bio:

Derek Peterson is an outdoor guy. He is not great at camping with his kids, but wants to learn more about it with others.
Categories:
Allerton Programs > All Ages Programs
The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Outdoor Education

Class Name: CANCELLED: Holistic End of Life Planning & Caregiving
Description:
Wednesdays*: 9/6, 10/4, 11/1, & 12/6

6:30-8:00pm

Studio (9/6 & 12/6) and Library (10/4 & 11/1)

$230/person total for all 4 classes

Registration Deadline: August 30th

Experience level: N/A

 

This is an ongoing class aimed to guide participants in becoming confident caregivers for loved ones facing the end of life, completing their own end of life planning, and being an apprentice to the mysteries of life and death. This is both practical and spiritual (though not faith-based), educational and experiential.

*Class meets the first Wednesday of the month from September to December. The registration fee is for all four classes.

 

Instructor Bio

Melanie is a registered nurse and a death, grief, and trauma guide, who's helped hundreds of humans move from fear and freeze into possibility and ease. Melanie founded ‘Threshold Doula’ in 2019 after 7 years as a cardiac and hospice nurse, helping people navigate the healthcare system and dying process with greater understanding and confidence. As a speaker, educator, & facilitator, she helps people have meaningful conversations about death and loss to foster the most peaceful, regret-free, empowered lives possible. Her clients include dying people, their families & care teams, nurses, and organizations who are amidst an existential reckoning or have experienced profound loss that has made a significant impact on their lives & communities. She is a passionate & skillful facilitator who loves to co-create the most right fit resources and experience for her clients.
Categories: The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Wellness

Description:
Tuesday, Dec. 12th, 5:00-6:00pm & 6:00-7:00pm

Thursday, Dec. 21st, 4:00-5:00pm & 5:00-6:00pm

Studio

$20/person, per timeslot

Registration Deadline: December 5th & December 14th

Experience level: beginner

 

Create a new type of holiday decoration that you can take home to add to your decor. All participants will receive a large white votive candle and use melted tea light candles of various colors to paint designs around the outside of the candle. Make it holiday themed, or paint designs that would work for a year-round decoration.

There are 4 chances to participate in this folk school class. Choose one (or 2) that best fits your schedule.

 

Instructor Bio

Olivia Bunting is the Senior Program Coordinator at Allerton Park & Retreat Center. She loves being outside, tending her plants while trying not to kill them, and crafting. The Farms gives her an excuse to learn new things and finish those half-done crafts she's been meaning to complete for a while.

Description:
Were you bummed when the August concert of the Allerton Concert Series was rained out? Us too! 

Join us at 7 p.m. Saturday, Nov. 18 in the Allerton Music Barn for a  rescheduled concert with Colt Ball & Friends! Originally scheduled to play at the Aug. 5 concert of the Allerton Concert Series, Colt and his band will be playing some groovy tunes to help you warm up on the chilly fall night. 

Doors open at 6:30 with a cash bar available throughout the concert. Bring your favorite concert snacks and join us on the 18th! 

$25/person. Space is limited.

If you will need disability-related accommodations in order to participate, please email owarren@illinois.edu.
Categories: Allerton Programs

Class Name: Creating an Individual Path to Wholeness 11/17
Description:
Friday, Nov 17th & Friday, Dec. 15th

10:30am – 2:00pm

Allerton Mansion Library (11/17)

Studio (12/15)

$140/class

Registration Deadline: November 13th & December 11th

Experience level: N/A

 

In a one day gathering, concepts of personal growth and wholeness are first learned through the use of film clips, art, poetry, myth etc. Then, through the use of writing, art, poetry, and music, each participant will work on their personal expression of wholeness. And finally, the participant shares their creation with the group if they choose.

 

Instructor Bio

Mary Ryan M.S. is a Jungian-oriented therapist with 40 years of experience working with individuals, couples, and groups. She has been on the faculty for 3 universities teaching classes to students from age 18 to 80 on personality and personal growth. With the Academy of Addictions Treatment Professionals, she trained therapists in Chicago and St. Louis. She has worked in both hospitals and treatment centers and has a private practice in Springfield, IL while also facilitating a Prison Fathers group at Jacksonville Correctional Center. Mary has had art displayed in two galleries and combines her experience in art and film with her work with clients and in her teaching.
Categories: The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Wellness

Description:
Saturday, October 7th

1:00 – 3:30pm

Auditorium

$20/student

Experience level: Beginner (youth 10-14yrs)

Registration Deadline: September 22nd

 

Creature Design for World Building and Storytelling is a class that introduces young people to the exciting creative exercise of creating new flora and fauna for science fiction and fantasy worlds. We will take a look at what creature design is, what its applications are, and how important science is to create a convincing world for entertaining others. The class will finish with a creative brainstorming session where each student will begin to create their own unique animal with habitat, food chain, biology, anatomy and story in mind.  All students must be able to draw. This is not an art class. Each student will receive a sketchbook and colored pencils to use and take home.

 

Instructor Bio

Ava Porter is going to be a senior in high school this fall. She has been drawing and creating new creatures and monsters since she was very small. She spends her free time writing and world-building along with many, many other creative hobbies. Ava has a passion for teaching others about animals and how to unlock creativity and is considering a career in zoology.

The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art

Description:
Thursday, Nov 16th

5:30 – 8:30pm

Mansion

$65/person

Registration Deadline: November 10th

Experience level: Beginner

 

During this class students will design and dye two scarves using resist techniques and indigo dye. Indigo is one of the oldest dyes used for textiles and printing. People from many different countries have utilized this organic compound to create a distinctive blue.

This workshop will familiarize you with a creative, multicultural practice that utilizes prominently plant-based materials. We will prepare an organic, fermentation dye vat utilizing indigo powder, an extract prepared from the plant Indigofera tinctoria. The indigo vat-making method is safe for (supervised) children to use. Shibori, a Japanese, manual, resist dyeing technique will be discussed. Utilizing basic shibori- inspired techniques, you will be able to create unique patterns on your scarves.

 

Instructor Bio

Jenny Knavel received her MFA from the University of Wisconsin-Madison in 1997. She is a professor of art at Western Illinois University in Macomb, Illinois, where she teaches primarily design and digital photography classes. In addition to teaching, she is an active artist. Her work has been juried into numerous regional and national and international juried exhibitions. In 2021, her artwork was selected to be on the cover of the SAQA Journal (Studio Art Quilt Associates), 2021, Volume 31, No. 2. Additionally, she had work included in the Second Biennial of Natural Dye exhibition, China Silk Museum, Hangzhou, China.
Categories: The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art

Description:
Join florist Rick Orr at Allerton Park for an introductory flower arranging class using fall florals. Students will learn about different floral design styles, and each student will prep foliage and flowers to create two beautiful arrangements to take home and enjoy.

Each student should bring a small sharp knife, a pair of pruners, and two vases. One vase should be a bowl approximately 3” tall and 6”-8” in diameter; the other should be a tall container approximately 10”-12” tall and 3-4” in diameter.

 

Instructor Bio:

Rick Orr has been offering classes in his floral studio for over 40 years, and has been in the industry for over 50. He is a member of the American Institute of Floral Designers and has 3 times been named Designer of the Year for Illinois. He was the official florist for the PGA Golf Tournament in Lake Zurich, IL, 1989, and has appeared on design panels in Texas, New York, Michigan, and Illinois. He was the guest curator for Petals and Paintings at the Krannert Art Museum for 25 years. His work has been featured in national magazines and books, as well as the Ralph Lauren shop in Chicago.
Categories: The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art

Description:
In this workshop for younger kids and their accompanying grown-ups, we'll experiment with different methods of making fall- and Halloween-themed art prints. Techniques will include printing with natural materials, as well as relief printing with kid-safe foam sheets. While all materials are washable, we recommend coming in clothes you don't mind getting a little messy! This workshop is intended for kids between the ages of 5-10, who must be accompanied by a parent or trusted adult.

The $35 registration fee covers one adult and one child.

Instructor Bio

Felix Fuenty (they/them) is Allerton's newest program coordinator. They like cats, the library, spicy food, Halloween, being outside, and all kinds of art.

Class Name: Fireside Fridays: Ghosts, Ghouls, and Grandma?!? (A Dia de los Muertos Celebration)
Description:
What better place to hear a ghost story than around the campfire at Allerton Park?!  Join us for Fireside Friday on Nov 3rd to learn about the traditions of Dia de los Muertos and the ghost story of La Llorona, the weeping woman, by Dr. Emma Velez from the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign.  Emma is an Assistant Professor working in both the Department of Gender and Women Studies and the Department of Latina/Latino Studies.  Attendees can create their own papel picado (punched paper) art, a decorative craft considered Mexican folk art, which are commonly displayed for both secular and religious occasions such as Dia de los Muertos (Day of the Dead).

Tickets are $5 per person, children 5 and under are free.  A cash bar will be open from 6-9pm.  S’mores will be served.  Please bring your own lawn chairs.
Categories:
Allerton Programs > All Ages Programs
Allerton Programs > Fireside Fridays

Class Name: Fireside Fridays: Spiders, Bats and Creepy Crawlies
Description:
One of the best things about fall is doing things around the campfire!  Come join us for Fireside Friday on Oct 13th for a talk all about spiders, bats, and blood-sucking insects with Dr. Holly Tuten from Prairie Research Institute at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign and Tommy McElrath of Illinois Natural History Survey.  Play a vector-borne disease game that will have you checking twice for ticks!

Tickets are $5 per person, children 5 and under are free.  A cash bar will be open from 5-8pm.  S’mores will be served.  Please bring your own lawn chairs.
Categories:
Allerton Programs > All Ages Programs
Allerton Programs > Fireside Fridays

Description:
One of the best things about summer is sitting around the campfire and gazing up at the stars!  Come join us for Fireside Friday on Sept 15th for a talk about the cosmos with UIUC professor and astrophysicist, Gautham Narayan.  We will do some stargazing and munch on s’mores.

Tickets are $5 per person, children 5 and under are free.  A cash bar will be open from 6-9pm. S’mores will be served.  Please bring your own lawn chairs.

5:00 – 7:00pm

Allerton Mansion Dining Room

$$/class

Registration Deadline: October 23rd & November 6th

Experience Level: Beginner

 

Learn the making of focaccia bread from scratch then ‘paint’ it with vegetables and herbs to make stunning yet edible designs.

 

Instructor Bio:

Leah Bodine calls herself the accidental chef with years of experience in the food industry. She started her catering company, Blue Dragonfly Catering, to express her creative mind in food preparation and cultivating recipes. She loves sharing her skills with others and seeing the fascination on students’ faces when they realize how creative they can be and how easy it is to do so. Her experiences in the food industry has led her to live in Paris and cater for rock and country legends.

Description:
*Registration for the GLOW All-Ages Scavenger Hunt is closed.  Missed it?  No problem!!  The clues are attached.GLOW All-Ages Scavenger Hunt Clues  Happy hunting!

**If you registered for this event, check your inbox - the clues have been sent as of 12/15 at 11am!

Want to add a little adventure to your Holiday GLOW experience?  Sign up today to participate in the GLOW All-Ages Scavenger Hunt starting December 15th.

Registration is FREE for the GLOW All-Ages Scavenger Hunt.  On December 15th a set of clues will be emailed to all registrants.  Use those clues at Allerton’s Holiday GLOW lighted walk any day between December 15th and 23rd from 5-9pm.  Post your pictures on Instagram with #AllGLOWnup.

$10/car suggested donation can be made upon entering the park, or donate online.  Donations are an important part of the success of the Holiday GLOW event.  Thank you for keeping this and other public programs continuing year after year!

If you require disability accommodations to participate in the event, please contact Sarah Putman at sputman2@illinois.edu at the time of registration.
Categories: Allerton Programs > All Ages Programs

Description:
Teens, join us for a selfie scavenger hunt through Allerton’s Holiday GLOW!

Gather a group of friends to create a team of 2-5 and race against other teams to snap photos of all the clues.  The first team back with photos of every clue will earn the ultimate bragging rights – being crowned the Allerton GLOW Getter!

Tickets are FREE for kids aged 13-18.  Parents are welcome to tag along but can also take a solo GLOW walk if desired.  Moon Bean Coffee truck will be on-site serving hot beverages (it’s cocoa season)!  The hunt will happen rain or shine so prepare for the weather and bring your own flashlight and a camera or phone.

Posting pictures on social media is not required, but if you post, we’d love for you to use #GLOWGetter to light up the Allerton Instagram feed.

If you require disability accommodations to participate in the event, please contact Sarah Putman at sputman2@illinois.edu at the time of registration.
Categories: Allerton Programs > Youth All-Day Programs

Description:
Get ready for a fun and festive holiday-themed event, where you will be able to blend your own scents to create a lovely festive candle. Professional candle artist Kayla Brown of Fire Doll Studio will lead you through a magical candle making journey that includes a fun variety of holiday scents to choose from. She will also provide some fun history, science, and techniques, along with her expertise as you make your own! Materials will be provided — including an apron — but dress accordingly, as we cannot guarantee you won’t end up with a little wax on your clothes.

NOTE: Your candle will require two hours of cure time, so will need to be picked up the next day; or it can be shipped to you for a fee of $8.
Categories:
The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art
Allerton Programs > Adult Programs

Description:
Come see the Allerton mansion twinkle and shine in all it's holiday splendor! You'll hear more about the unique history of Robert Allerton and the creation of his estate at one of our Mansion Tours - holiday style!

 

The tour begins at 3pm in the Gallery of the Mansion.  Tours typically last between 45 and 60 minutes.

$15/person, ages 5 and under are free

Space is limited and tours typically sell out.

(NOTE: If tickets are sold out and you need additional children’s tickets, email Sarah Putman at sputman2@illinois.edu.)

If you will need disability-related accommodations in order to participate, please email Sarah Putman at sputman2@illinois.edu.

Description:
Registration deadline: December 6, 2023

 

Urbana-based local artist Kari Keller of Kari’s Occasions will demonstrate simple but fun techniques to paint and decorate glass bulb ornaments. Participants will create their own keepsake ornament for the holidays.

 

Instructor Bio: My name is Kari Keller. I have been an arts and crafts enthusiast since I was a very young child. I have a lot of experience in drawing and painting, and have dabbled in a large variety of other art forms including macramé, perler bead art, cake topper design, painting ceramic plates, acrylic pouring, and digital art. I also have a thriving side business as a face painter for these past 8 years. I love trying new things and making new friends!
Categories: The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art

Description:
Delight Flower Farm and Allerton Park welcome you to experience the joys of the winter spirit through evergreen wreath making. This workshop will offer participants the opportunity to craft a unique, seasonal decoration. You will learn basic wreath building and design techniques, and will use locally-sourced supplies to make a beautiful, handmade treasure. Wreath decor includes fresh evergreen, berries, pine cones, tree nuts, ribbons, dried flowers, and more.

Each participant will go home with a holiday wreath! Classes are non-refundable and non-transferable. Class will be building wreaths on an 12” frame (size is more like 20” diameter when finished). A cash bar will be available during this class.

Instructor Bio: Maggie Taylor, Delight Flower Farm owner, has been farming flowers and leading workshops since 2011. Trained as an artist, she loves turning natural materials into objects of joy and empowering others to do it too. Depending on the time of year, she may be teaching about fresh flowers or winter evergreen wreaths. Maggie's teaching style is accessible, informative, encouraging and often humorous! Other capable farm crew members assist in teaching large groups.
Categories: The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art

Class Name: Improve Your Improv (Entire Series - for attendees from 9/14)
Description:
**Interested in taking all Improve Your Improv classes? Use this registration link!

Thursdays: 9/21, 9/28, 10/5, 10/12, 10/19, 10/26, 11/2

6:30 – 8:00pm

Music Barn

$20/class

Registration Deadline: September 11th, and every consecutive Monday until October 30th

Experience level: Beginner

Art, Class, Multiple Sessions

 

The “Improve Your Improv” class teaches participants of all experience levels the mechanics and tools used to create improvisational theater.   This class will be jam-packed with improvisational fun. The class centers around long-form improv - scene creation, learning the essentials, and team dynamics.  However, short-form improv games will also be used to learn the craft.  The games and practices used will help all levels of improvisers develop a skillset and become familiar with the world of Improv! This will not be a class to sit down, hear lectures, and take notes.  Students will be performing as much as possible! it will be a get up and perform kind of class. The class will emphasize the mechanics needed to act out an entertaining and meaningful scene on the spot with no lines. While improv is often a natural skill, there are many elements of the craft that can be taught to everybody.  These skills are useful on stage but also in everyday life, wherever you might be put on the spot!

 

Instructor Bio:

Kyle is an improvisor, improv teacher, filmmaker, writer, comedian, actor and musician from the Chicago suburbs. He started performing improv in 2004 and has been improvising in front of audiences regularly since 2015. He has also taught classes both in-person and remote at Westside Improv in Wheaton, Illinois and is a member of multiple active improv troupes. He performs live across the state in various comedy and music acts and has produced hundreds of videos, short films, music videos and songs.

The class will emphasize the mechanics needed to act out an entertaining and meaningful scene on the spot with no lines. While improv is often a natural skill, there are many elements of the craft that can be taught to everybody.  These skills are useful on stage but also in everyday life, wherever you might be put on the spot!

**Note: While registration for the entire series is not required, it is highly suggested that all students attend each class to get the full experience. However, students can pick sessions that work best with their schedule if needed. A promo-code will be given to those registering for all 8 classes. Please email Olivia at owarren@illinois.edu for more information.

 

Instructor Bio:

Kyle is an improvisor, improv teacher, filmmaker, writer, comedian, actor and musician from the Chicago suburbs. He started performing improv in 2004 and has been improvising in front of audiences regularly since 2015. He has also taught classes both in-person and remote at Westside Improv in Wheaton, Illinois and is a member of multiple active improv troupes. He performs live across the state in various comedy and music acts and has produced hundreds of videos, short films, music videos and songs.
Categories: The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art

Description:
**Interested in taking all Intro to Bookbinding classes? Use this registration link!**

Tuesdays: 11/7, 11/14, 11/21, 11/28

6:00 – 8:00pm

Allerton Mansion Library

$90/class

Registration Deadline: October 23rd 

Experience level: Beginner

 

These classes are an introduction to different approaches to bookbinding, both for practical and artistic purposes. By the end of the course, students would have a complete understanding of the parts of a book, methods of bookbinding, and the purpose and function of each binding type. Students would leave with a book demonstrating each binding method learned, as well as a final book project demonstrating an individual creative approach synthesizing the binding methods taught in class.

 

Instructor Bio

Arthur is a local artist with a BFA with Honors in New Media from the University of Illinois and works with a multitude of media, from stop-motion, to code; from knitting, to bookbinding. Arthur currently works for the University of Illinois Library in its Preservation Department: repairing, rebinding, and binding books, hosting hands-on binding outreach events, and other preservation practices.

Class Name: Learn the Guitar: Get Started the Right Way
Description:
Fridays: 9/15, 9/22, 10/6, 10/13, 10/20, 10/27, 11/3, 11/10, 11/17, 12/1

5:30 – 6:30pm

Studio

$300 for all 10 classes ($30/class)

Registration Deadline: September 8th

Experience level: Beginner

 

Rex Parvin has a very specific approach to teaching guitar based on having an understanding—using simple terminology—of how music works and how that understanding applies specifically to the guitar.  It's essentially a 3-step process.

 

**Note: This class meets on Fridays September-December (please note skipped weeks). The registration fee is for all 10 classes and students should be present for all classes.

 

Instructor Bio

Rex knew from early adolescence that his primary passion in life was music, having taken guitar lessons and learning to read music starting at age 5. He learned how to conduct choirs in high school and went to college as a voice major, getting bachelor’s and master’s degrees in music education, specializing in voice and conducting. Rex’s musical abilities and passions led to a 30-year career as a full-time music director, studio musician, conductor, and guitar teacher. He is a “people person” that loves to interact with others and see them grow in their abilities and understanding of music, encouraging them to new levels of excellence. Those who have learned from him have always commented that they learned many new things in ways that were easily understandable and created a hunger to learn even more.
Categories: The Farms: An Allerton Folk School > Art

Class Name: OSES: “Reparations: Universalism and the African American Struggle for Autonomy”
Description:
Join Dr. Sundiata Cha-Jua for the next presentation in the Olive Spannaus Education Series, “Reparations: Universalism and the African American Struggle for Autonomy” at 6 p.m. Wednesday, Nov. 8 in the Mansion Library.

Dr. Cha-Jua is a professor of African-American Studies and History at the University of Illinois. After his talk, there will be a Q&A.

$5/person. Register by Nov. 7. All sales are final.

If you will need disability-related accommodations to participate, please email owarren@illinois.edu.

About Dr. Sundiata Cha-Jua

Sundiata Keita Cha-Jua teaches in the departments of African American Studies and History at the University of Illinois.

He authored America’s First Black Town, Brooklyn, Illinois, 1830-1915; Sankofa: Racial Formation and Transformation, Toward a Theory of African American History and co-edited Race Struggles with Theodore Koditschek and Helen Neville. He has also published scores of articles in leading journals.

Cha-Jua has been President of the National Council for Black Studies (2011-2012, 2012-2014), Senior Editor of The Black Scholar (2011-2015) and Associate Editor of the Journal of African American History (2015-2018). Since September 2015, he has written a biweekly Op-Ed commentary, “RealTalk: A Black Perspective” for the News-Gazette of Champaign, Illinois.

Dr. Cha-Jua is a founding scholar/trainer of the Policing in a Multiracial Society Program (PSMP).  Started in 2012, PSMP provides systematic anti-racial bias education and training for police recruits attending the University of Illinois’s Police Training Institute (PTI) and researches the racial attitudes of police and the effectiveness of anti-racist training.

Cha-Jua has been engaged with local and national Black liberation movement organizations since his teen years. He has been a member of the executive board of the St. Louis-based Organization for Black Struggle (OBS), a member of the National Council of the Black Radical Congress (BRC) and is an organizer for the Malcolm X Grassroots Movement (MXGM).

Olive Spannaus Education Series

Made possible through a gift from Fred Spannaus and Connie Requarth in memory of Olive Wise Spannaus (1916-2018), the Olive Spannaus Education Series highlights underrepresented communities in art, nature, and history, through sessions led by members of those communities.

Description:
Join us for an enchanting evening dedicated to owls at Owl Night, 6 to 9 p.m. Wednesday, Nov. 1 in the Greenhouse Auditorium. This night will be centered around the fascinating Northern Saw Whet Owls, as researchers conduct live banding of these migrating avian wonders.

Don't miss this rare opportunity to get close up with these elusive creatures. Bring the little ones along for a host of engaging kids’ activities that promise both fun and learning. As the night unfolds, enjoy the camaraderie at our cash bar as you mingle with fellow owl enthusiasts.

$10/person (5 & under free). All sales are final.

You can learn more about ongoing research at the park here.

Complete your visit with a discounted overnight stay ($100/room) in the Mansion or one of our guesthouses. View rooms here and make a reservation online or by calling 217-333-3287.

Description:
Have you ever wandered around Allerton Park and wondered what the inside of the historic mansion looks like?  Would you like to hear more about the unique history of Robert Allerton and the creation of his estate?  Register for one of our Public Mansion Tours held each month!

 

Mansion tours start at 2pm and begin in the Gallery of the Mansion.  Tours typically last between 45 and 60 minutes.

 

$10/person, ages 5 and under are free

Space is limited and tours typically sell out.

(NOTE: If tickets are sold out and you need additional children’s tickets, email Sarah Putman at sputman2@illinois.edu.)

 

If you will need disability-related accommodations in order to participate, please email Sarah Putman at sputman2@illinois.edu.

Description:
Simmer Pots are a wonderful way to add safe chemical-free scents to your home, but have you ever considered their other properties? We will also explore their use as a mindfulness tool. Then we will move our discussion to the fruits, herbs, flowers and spices that we intentionally choose to add and what properties those can bring into our lives as well. This is a hands-on workshop where you will be invited to interact with fresh ingredients with several different pots, and will take home a starter kit and sample. There will be time to ask questions and learn from each other!

Instructor Bio: Jessica Howard is the owner of Hearth Haven Crafts and More. I have always loved the idea of incorporating nature into my art and crafts. My home has always been my sanctuary, my ever-changing alter to nature and family. I began working with simmer pots 3 years ago when I was looking for a way to bring a shared positive mindfulness kitchen experience with my daughter. I shared this experience with a group of friends and found that it was a great way to gather with intention. There's an aspect of simmer pots for everyone, I hope to offer suggestions and let you take from it what you need.
